The ***Clemson* class** was a series of 156 [destroyers](/wiki/Destroyer "Destroyer") (six more were cancelled and never begun) built at the end of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the majority of which served with the [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") from after World War I and through [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

The *Clemson*-class ships were commissioned by the United States Navy from 1919 to 1922, built by [Newport News Shipbuilding & Dry Dock Company](/wiki/Northrop_Grumman_Shipbuilding "Northrop Grumman Shipbuilding"), [New York Shipbuilding Corporation](/wiki/New_York_Shipbuilding_Corporation "New York Shipbuilding Corporation"), [William Cramp & Sons](/wiki/William_Cramp_%26_Sons "William Cramp & Sons"), [Bethlehem Steel Corporation](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel_Corporation "Bethlehem Steel Corporation"), [Mare Island Naval Shipyard](/wiki/Mare_Island_Naval_Shipyard "Mare Island Naval Shipyard"), [Norfolk Naval Shipyard](/wiki/Norfolk_Naval_Shipyard "Norfolk Naval Shipyard") and [Bath Iron Works](/wiki/Bath_Iron_Works "Bath Iron Works"), some quite rapidly. The *Clemson* class was a minor redesign of the [*Wickes* class](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer") for greater fuel capacity and was the last pre-World War II class of [flush-deck](/wiki/Flush-deck "Flush-deck") destroyers to be built for the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). Until the [*Fletcher*-class destroyer](/wiki/Fletcher-class_destroyer "Fletcher-class destroyer"), the *Clemson*s were the most numerous class of destroyers commissioned in the United States Navy and were known colloquially as "flush-deckers", "four-stackers" or "four-pipers".

## Design evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clemson-class_destroyer&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Design evolution")]

As finally built, the *Clemson* class would be a fairly straightforward expansion of the [*Wickes*-class destroyers](/wiki/Wickes-class_destroyer "Wickes-class destroyer"). While the *Wickes* class had given good service there was a desire to build a class more tailored towards the anti-submarine role, and as such several design studies were completed, mainly about increasing the ships' range. These designs included a reduction in speed to between 26–28 [knots](/wiki/Knot_(unit) "Knot (unit)") (48–52 km/h; 30–32 mph) by eliminating two boilers, freeing up displacement for [depth charges](/wiki/Depth_charge "Depth charge") and more fuel. This proposal foreshadowed the [destroyer escorts](/wiki/Destroyer_escort "Destroyer escort") of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Clemson-class_destroyer_inboard_and_ouboard_profiles_1920s.png/250px-Clemson-class_destroyer_inboard_and_ouboard_profiles_1920s.png)](/wiki/File:Clemson-class_destroyer_inboard_and_ouboard_profiles_1920s.png)

Upgrading the gun armament from 4-inch (102 mm) to 5-inch (127 mm) guns was also considered, but only five ships (DD-231 to DD-235) were armed with 5-inch guns. In addition, the tapered stern of the *Wickes*-class destroyers resulted in a large turning radius and a correction to this defect was also sought, although this was not corrected in the final design. In the end the General Board decided the 35 knots (65 km/h; 40 mph) speed be retained so as to allow the *Clemson* class to be used as a fleet escort. The pressing need for destroyers overruled any change that would slow production compared to the proceeding *Wickes* class. Wing tanks for [fuel oil](/wiki/Fuel_oil "Fuel oil") were installed on either side of the ships to increase the operational range.[[3]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._44-3) This design choice meant the fuel oil would be stored above the waterline and create additional vulnerability, but the Navy felt a 4,900-[nautical-mile](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") (9,100 km; 5,600 mi) range was worth the risk.[[3]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._44-3) Additional improvements included provisions for 5-inch guns to be installed at a later date, an enlarged rudder to help reduce the turn radius, and an additional [3-inch (76 mm) anti-aircraft gun](/wiki/3%22/23_caliber_gun "3\"/23 caliber gun") on the after deck-house.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The class resulted from a [General Board](/wiki/General_Board_of_the_United_States_Navy "General Board of the United States Navy") recommendation for further destroyers to combat the submarine threat, culminating in a total of 267 *Wickes*- and *Clemson*-class destroyers completed. However, the design of the ships remained optimized for operation with the [battleship](/wiki/Battleship "Battleship") fleet.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Armament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clemson-class_destroyer&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Armament")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/USS_Lamson_%28DD-328%29.jpg/250px-USS_Lamson_%28DD-328%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Lamson_(DD-328).jpg)

Overhead view of [USS *Lamson*](/wiki/USS_Lamson_(DD-328) "USS Lamson (DD-328)")

The main armament was the same as the *Wickes* class: four [4-inch (102 mm)/50 caliber guns](/wiki/4%22/50_caliber_gun "4\"/50 caliber gun") and twelve 21-inch (533 mm) [torpedo tubes](/wiki/Torpedo_tubes "Torpedo tubes"). The [Mark 8 torpedo](/wiki/Mark_8_torpedo "Mark 8 torpedo") was initially equipped, and probably remained the standard torpedo for this class, as 600 Mark 8 torpedoes were issued to the British in 1940 as part of the [Destroyers for Bases Agreement](/wiki/Destroyers_for_Bases_Agreement "Destroyers for Bases Agreement").[[6]](#cite_note-navyhist-6)

Although the design provided for two [anti-aircraft](/wiki/Anti-aircraft "Anti-aircraft") (AA) guns, most ships carried a single [3-inch (76 mm)/23 caliber](/wiki/3%22/23_caliber_gun "3\"/23 caliber gun") AA gun, typically on the aft deckhouse. A frequent modification was replacing the aft 4-inch gun with the 3-inch gun to make more room for the [depth charge](/wiki/Depth_charge "Depth charge") tracks.[[3]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._44-3) [Anti-submarine](/wiki/Anti-submarine "Anti-submarine") (ASW) armament was added during or after construction. Typically, two depth charge tracks were provided aft, along with a [Y-gun](/wiki/Y-gun "Y-gun") depth charge projector forward of the aft deckhouse.[[7]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._45-7)

Despite the provision for 5-inch guns, only seven ships were built with an increased gun armament. [USS *Hovey*](/wiki/USS_Hovey "USS Hovey") and [USS *Long*](/wiki/USS_Long "USS Long") had twin 4-inch/50 mounts for a total of eight guns, while DD 231–235 had four [5-inch (127 mm)/51 caliber guns](/wiki/5%22/51_caliber_gun "5\"/51 caliber gun") in place of the 4-inch guns.

## In operation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clemson-class_destroyer&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: In operation")]

As with the preceding *Wickes* class, the fleet found that the tapered cruiser stern, which made for a nice depth charge deployment feature, dug into the water and increased the turning radius, thus hampering [anti-submarine](/wiki/Anti-submarine "Anti-submarine") work.[[8]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._46-8)[[9]](#cite_note-destroyerhistory.org-9) While an increased rudder size helped, the answer would be in a redesigned stern, but this was not implemented. They were reported to be prone to heavy rolling in light load conditions.[[7]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._45-7) The flush deck gave the hull great strength but this also made the deck very wet.[[8]](#cite_note-Friedman,_p._46-8)[[9]](#cite_note-destroyerhistory.org-9)

## Ships in class

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clemson-class_destroyer&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ships in class")]

Main article: [List of Clemson-class destroyers](/wiki/List_of_Clemson-class_destroyers "List of Clemson-class destroyers")

156 *Clemson*-class destroyers were built (with hull numbers DD 186 to 199 and DD 206 to 347), with an additional six (DD 200 to 205) cancelled.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Clemson-class_destroyer&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/NH_66721_Honda_Point.gif/250px-NH_66721_Honda_Point.gif)](/wiki/File:NH_66721_Honda_Point.gif)

The Honda Point Disaster 1923.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/US_flushdeck_destroyer_base_1924.jpg/250px-US_flushdeck_destroyer_base_1924.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_flushdeck_destroyer_base_1924.jpg)

Seventy-seven "four-stackers" laid up at San Diego in 1924.

Only 64 of this class had been begun by Armistice Day, 11 November 1918, and none had yet been commissioned.

[USS *Graham*](/wiki/USS_Graham "USS Graham") collided with SS *Panama* off the New Jersey coast on 16 December 1921 and had to return to New York, where she was decommissioned on 31 March 1922, and was sold for scrapping on 19 September 1922.

Fourteen ships of the class were involved in the [Honda Point Disaster](/wiki/Honda_Point_Disaster "Honda Point Disaster") (a.k.a. Point Pedernales) in September 1923, of which seven were lost in the worst peacetime loss in US Naval history.

Many never saw wartime service, as a significant number were decommissioned in 1930 and scrapped as part of the [London Naval Treaty](/wiki/London_Naval_Treaty "London Naval Treaty"). About 40 *Clemson*-class destroyers with [Yarrow boilers](/wiki/Yarrow_boiler "Yarrow boiler") were scrapped or otherwise disposed of in 1930–31, as these boilers wore out quickly in service. Flush-deckers in reserve were commissioned as replacements.[[11]](#cite_note-11) In 1936 only some 169 of the flush deck destroyers would be left, four *Caldwell* class and the rest *Wickes* and *Clemson* class.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1937 four *Clemson* class were converted to [destroyer minelayers](/wiki/Destroyer_minelayer "Destroyer minelayer") ([hull classification symbol](/wiki/Hull_classification_symbol "Hull classification symbol") DM), joining several *Wickes*-class ships in this role.

Nineteen were transferred to the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") in 1940 as part of the [Destroyers for Bases Agreement](/wiki/Destroyers_for_Bases_Agreement "Destroyers for Bases Agreement"), where they became part of the [Town class](/wiki/Town-class_destroyer "Town-class destroyer"). Others were upgraded or converted to [high-speed transports](/wiki/High-speed_transport "High-speed transport") (APD), high-speed [minesweepers](/wiki/Minesweeper "Minesweeper") (DMS), [destroyer minelayers](/wiki/Destroyer_minelayer "Destroyer minelayer") (DM), or [seaplane tenders](/wiki/Seaplane_tender "Seaplane tender") (AVD) and served through [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Four *Wickes*-class DM conversions and the four *Clemson*-class DM conversions survived to serve in World War II.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Clemson-class_destroyer_sinking_c1942.jpg/250px-Clemson-class_destroyer_sinking_c1942.jpg)](/wiki/File:Clemson-class_destroyer_sinking_c1942.jpg)

*Clemson*-class destroyer (possibly [USS *Pope*](/wiki/USS_Pope_(DD-225) "USS Pope (DD-225)")) sinking, c. 1942

Most ships remaining in service during World War II were rearmed with dual-purpose [3-inch/50 caliber guns](/wiki/3%22/50_caliber_gun "3\"/50 caliber gun") to provide better anti-aircraft protection.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The AVD [seaplane tender](/wiki/Seaplane_tender "Seaplane tender") conversions received two guns; the APD high-speed transport, DM [minelayer](/wiki/Minelayer "Minelayer"), and DMS minesweeper conversions received three guns, and those retaining destroyer classification received six.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Their original low-angle [4-inch/50 caliber guns](/wiki/4%22/50_caliber_gun "4\"/50 caliber gun") (Mark 9) were transferred to [Defensively Equipped Merchant Ships](/wiki/Defensively_Equipped_Merchant_Ships "Defensively Equipped Merchant Ships") for anti-submarine protection.[[15]](#cite_note-15) For the ships converted to minesweepers, the twelve 21-inch torpedo tubes were replaced by minesweeping gear.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[USS *Stewart*](/wiki/USS_Stewart_(DD-224) "USS Stewart (DD-224)") was scuttled at [Soerabaja](/wiki/Surabaya "Surabaya") on 2 March 1942, following the surrender of the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies") to the Japanese. She was raised, repaired and recommissioned as [Japanese patrol boat *PB-102*](/wiki/USS_Stewart_(DD-224)#Imperial_Japanese_Navy "USS Stewart (DD-224)") by the [Imperial Japanese Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Navy "Imperial Japanese Navy"). She was surrendered to the US Navy following the end of World War II and was used as a target for aircraft. In addition, 17 *Clemson*-class destroyers were lost during the war.

The wrecks of three *Clemson*-class destroyers remain in the [San Francisco Bay area](/wiki/San_Francisco_Bay_area "San Francisco Bay area"), [USS *Corry*](/wiki/USS_Corry_(DD-334) "USS Corry (DD-334)") a few miles north of [Mare Island Navy Yard](/wiki/Mare_Island_Navy_Yard "Mare Island Navy Yard") on the [Napa River](/wiki/Napa_River "Napa River"), [USS *Thompson*](/wiki/USS_Thompson_(DD-305) "USS Thompson (DD-305)") in the southern part of the Bay where it was used as a bombing target in World War II,[[17]](#cite_note-17) and the aforementioned USS *Stewart* in what is now the Cordell Bank National Marine Sanctuary.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

A number of ships in the class were christened by the initial batch of women who enlisted in the Navy as [Yeoman (F)](/wiki/Yeoman_(F) "Yeoman (F)") in World War I. [USS *Hatfield*](/wiki/USS_Hatfield "USS Hatfield") was sponsored by Mrs. J. Edmond Haugh (Helen Brooks) who had been a Yeoman during World War I.
